metadata.title: Why String Similarity Is Not Semantic Equivalence (and How to Catch the Difference Before You Ship)

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Jambo, Liam — in for Bear. Why wasn't it? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.segment: Why String Similarity Is Not Semantic

beats.narration_text: Seth has a classifier. Twelve test pairs, 85% accuracy, all pass. The code is clean. He almost ships. Then he types one more pair by hand: hunting versus haunting. The function returns 0.93 — nearly identical. Hunting means the ghost is chasing a player. Haunting is the ambient-presence state. They are not the same state. They share six letters.

on_screen: Why was it not ready to ship?
beats.narration_text: An 85% accurate classifier that passes all 12 tests should be reliable enough to ship. Why wasn't it?

The function Claude wrote does exactly what Seth asked for. He asked for a string similarity scorer. He got a string similarity scorer. 12 test pairs, 85% accuracy, all sensible. The function is correct inside the frame Seth gave it. The frame was the wrong frame. He needed semantic equivalence over a fixed taxonomy — whether two tags describe the same NPC state. He asked for string similarity — whether two strings share letters. Those are not the same problem.

on_screen: Tests verify what the function does.
Not what you needed it to do.
beats.narration_text: The test suite only confirmed the problem Claude was asked to solve. String similarity on obvious variants — hunting versus hunting, patrolling versus patrolling — passes. But the test set had no pairs where the strings are similar and the states are different. Claude wrote tests for the function it built. Seth needed tests for the problem he was trying to solve. Those are not the same set.

Seth was forty minutes in. The code compiled. He almost shipped. What stopped him was not a test failure. It was an itch — a small, hard-to-articulate wrongness located between his eye and the screen. He stared at the test pairs. They all involved obvious things. He typed hunting versus haunting by hand. Not because a test told him to. Because his domain knowledge told him that was the hardest case.

The function returned 0.93. Hunting and haunting share six letters in a seven-letter window. By string similarity, they are nearly the same word. In the game's behavior tree, they are opposite states — one active-chase, one ambient-presence. The classifier would have merged them. The AI director would have received corrupted state data. The bug would have shipped. No test in the suite would have caught it, because no test in the suite asked: what is the hardest case for a string-similarity scorer on short state tags?

The move Seth made has a name: plausibility auditing. It is the capacity to feel that something is off before any test confirms it. Not gut feeling — trained perception. Domain knowledge applied to Claude's output. The itch is real. It is what the expert feels before they can articulate the rule that was violated.

Leila is building a quiz-answer grader. Claude uses fuzzy string matching with a threshold of 0.6. All ten test answers pass. She runs one more: the student wrote photosynthesis. The correct answer is cellular respiration. Similarity score: 0.67 — above the threshold. The grader marks it correct. String similarity doesn't know biology. Leila's domain knowledge does. She ran the probe before shipping because she knew biology.

Plausibility auditing is a practice, not a feeling. Before you ship a classifier — or any output Claude produces — ask: what is the hardest case for this approach, given what I know about the domain? Write that case. Run it. If the result feels wrong, it probably is wrong, even if the test suite says otherwise. The domain knowledge that identifies the hard case is yours. Claude does not have it.

on_screen: Tests verify the problem Claude solved.
Auditing verifies the problem you needed to solve.
beats.narration_text: Tests verify the problem Claude solved — not the problem you needed to solve. The function did exactly what the spec said. The spec was the wrong frame. Plausibility auditing is the capacity that catches the wrong frame before any test is ready to confirm it — and it only works if you bring domain knowledge Claude doesn't have.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. Leila is building a quiz-answer grader. Claude uses fuzzy string matching with a threshold of 0.6. All ten test answers pass. She runs one more: the student wrote photosynthesis. The correct answer is cellular respiration. Similarity score: 0.67 — above the threshold. The grader marks it correct. String similarity doesn't know biology. Leila's domain knowledge does. She ran the probe before shipping because she knew biology. Tests verify the problem Claude solved — not the problem you needed to solve. The function did exactly what the spec said. The spec was the wrong frame. Plausibility auditing is the capacity that catches the wrong frame before any test is ready to confirm it — and it only works if you bring domain knowledge Claude doesn't have.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
